1: # Bidstand — Product Requirements Document
2: 
3: > Implements **Option 3: Mini Realtime Auction Room** from the take-home assignment.
4: > This document is the seed spec — paste it into `/gsd-new-project` or hand it to whatever
5: > AI coding tool you're driving.
6: 
7: ## 0. One-liner
8: 
9: A realtime auction room where a **Commissioner** runs a live player auction and **Team
10: Owners** bid against each other, under a fixed budget, for a fixed pool of players —
11: until every player is sold or marked unsold.
12: 
13: ## 1. Chosen Domain & Core Assumption
14: 
15: The brief explicitly references `iplauction` as the spiritual model. We are building a
16: **cricket fantasy franchise player auction** (IPL-style):
17: 
18: - Items being auctioned = **Players** (name, role/category, base price, optional photo/stats)
19: - Bidders = **Team Owners**, each representing a **Franchise** with a fixed **Purse**
20:   (budget) and a squad with a max size + role quotas
21: 
22: This is the **stated assumption** the brief asks for. The schema is written generically
23: (`Item`, not `CricketPlayer`) so the same app could be relabeled for sneakers, art, or
24: domain names with no schema change — only seed data and copy would change.
25: 
26: Currency is stored as an **integer in Lakhs** (₹1,00,000 = 1 unit; ₹1 Crore = 100 units)
27: to avoid floating-point bugs. Display formatting (₹X.XX Cr) happens only in the UI layer.
28: 
29: ## 2. Roles
30: 
31: | Role | Description | Identity |
32: |---|---|---|
33: | **Commissioner** (Admin) | Creates rooms, defines the player pool & team purses, starts/pauses/resumes/ends the auction, can force-resolve a stuck item | Real account — email + password |
34: | **Team Owner** (Participant) | Claims one team in a room, bids on the currently active player | Room-scoped session — display name + room code, no account needed |
35: | **Spectator** | Joins to watch only, no bidding rights | Room-scoped session, no team claim |
36: 
37: Why no account for Team Owners: friction-free joining is core to the "feels live" goal —
38: nobody should have to sign up mid-auction to bid. The room-scoped session still
39: satisfies the "auth or session identity" requirement, and it's the realistic UX pattern
40: for this kind of room (Kahoot/Jackbox-style join, not a SaaS login).
41: 
42: ## 3. Goals
43: 
44: - The auction **must feel live**: bids, the current player, the timer, and presence all
45:   update across every open browser tab with no manual refresh.
46: - The auction logic (timer expiry, resolution, validation) is **server-authoritative**.
47:   No client ever decides an outcome; it only ever proposes a bid and renders state the
48:   server pushes back.
49: - The product works end-to-end for a full auction with zero developer hand-holding:
50:   create room → add players & teams → share code → run auction → see results.
51: 
52: ## 4. Non-Goals (v1)
53: 
54: - Mobile responsiveness (assignment explicitly says desktop-only is fine).
55: - Payments / real money — purse is play-money only.
56: - Multi-auction tournaments, trade windows, retained players from "last season."
57: - A public room directory — all rooms are private-by-code by design.
58: 
59: ## 5. Core Features (from the assignment, mapped to this product)
60: 
61: | Brief requirement | Implementation |
62: |---|---|
63: | Create auction room | Commissioner sets name, purse per team, default players, increment rule, timer length |
64: | Join room by code/link | 6-character room code + shareable `/join/[code]` link |
65: | Admin and participant roles | Commissioner / Team Owner / Spectator (see §2) |
66: | Item/player list | Player pool with name, role, base price, photo (optional), status |
67: | Start auction | Commissioner action, moves room `LOBBY → AUCTION` |
68: | Current item/player display | Large "on the block" card, visible to everyone in the room |
69: | Countdown timer | Server-owned absolute `timerEndsAt`; resets on every valid new bid |
70: | Realtime bidding | Socket-based; server validates and broadcasts every accepted bid |
71: | Bid history | Per-player live list: team, amount, timestamp |
72: | Sold/unsold outcome | Timer expiry with no bid → `UNSOLD`; expiry with a leading bid → `SOLD` |
73: | Final results page | Per-team squad, total spend, purse remaining; full unsold list |
74: | Basic room state persistence | Every state transition is written to Postgres, not just kept in memory |
75: 
76: ## 6. Optional Features Included in v1
77: 
78: Chosen because they reinforce the "feels like a real auction" goal and are cheap once
79: sockets + Postgres exist:
80: 
81: - **Team/squad budgets** — purse decreases as a team wins players; can't bid past remaining purse.
82: - **Maximum players per team** — squad size cap (default 18, configurable).
83: - **Role/category caps** — min/max per role (e.g. Batsman/Bowler/All-rounder/Wicketkeeper), configurable per room.
84: - **Auction pause/resume** — Commissioner can pause mid-item; timer freezes and resumes from remaining time.
85: - **Presence indicators** — who's currently connected in the room (small thing, ships almost free once sockets exist).
86: - **Spectator mode** — join without claiming a team.
87: 
88: **Explicitly deferred** to "Future Improvements" (see Known Limitations in README):
89: skip/withdraw voting, chat/reactions, public room directory.
90: 
91: ## 7. Auction State Machine
92: 
93: ```
94: LOBBY ──(commissioner starts)──▶ AUCTION ──(all items resolved)──▶ COMPLETED
95:                                     │  ▲
96:                           (pause)   ▼  │ (resume)
97:                                   PAUSED
98: ```
99: 
100: Within `AUCTION`, each player goes through its own micro state machine:
101: 
102: ```
103: PENDING ──(comes up next)──▶ IN_AUCTION ──(timer expiry, no bid)────▶ UNSOLD
104:                                   │
105:                                   └────(timer expiry, has leading bid)──▶ SOLD
106: ```
107: 
108: Rules:
109: - Exactly one item is `IN_AUCTION` at a time.
110: - A bid is only accepted if: item is `IN_AUCTION`, room is `AUCTION` (not `PAUSED`),
111:   bidder's team has enough remaining purse, bid ≥ current price + required increment,
112:   and the bid arrives before `timerEndsAt`.
113: - Every accepted bid **resets** `timerEndsAt` to `now + timerDuration` (classic
114:   "going once, going twice" pattern). There's a configurable max reset count per item
115:   to guarantee items can't be bid-warred forever (default: unlimited, override-able).
116: - Commissioner can **force-resolve** an item (mark sold to current high bid, or unsold)
117:   if something gets stuck — an escape hatch, not the happy path.
118: 
119: ## 8. Functional Requirements by Screen
120: 
121: **Auth / Landing** — Commissioner sign up / log in. Loading state on submit, inline
122: validation errors, redirect to dashboard on success.
123: 
124: **Commissioner Dashboard** — list of rooms created, "Create Room" CTA, room status
125: badges (Lobby/Live/Completed).
126: 
127: **Create Room Wizard** — room name, default purse per team, number of teams, timer
128: duration, increment rule (flat or tiered — see Architecture doc), squad size cap, role
129: caps, then add players (manual entry or paste/import a list — CSV minimum). Empty state
130: before any players are added; can't start auction with zero players or zero teams.
131: 
132: **Lobby** — room code + shareable link prominently displayed, live list of joined teams
133: (name, owner display name, connected/disconnected dot), Commissioner-only "Start
134: Auction" button (disabled until ≥1 team has joined).
135: 
136: **Auction Room (the core screen)** — current player card (name, role, base price, photo
137: placeholder if none), current highest bid + leading team, countdown ring/bar, bid
138: button(s) per the increment rule, live bid history feed, all teams' purse-remaining +
139: squad-fill bar, Commissioner-only pause/resume/force-resolve controls.
140: 
141: **Results Page** — per-team final squad with prices paid, purse spent vs. remaining,
142: full list of unsold players, shareable/printable summary. Updates live if viewed while
143: the auction is still finishing the last item.
144: 
145: Every screen needs explicit **loading**, **empty**, and **error** states — this is a
146: named evaluation criterion, not a nice-to-have.
147: 
148: ## 9. Non-Functional Requirements
149: 
150: - **Realtime correctness across tabs**: open the same room in 3+ browser tabs/profiles,
151:   bid from each, and all tabs must converge to the same state within ~1 round trip.
152: - **Concurrency**: two teams bidding within the same animation frame must resolve
153:   deterministically (server processes one room's events sequentially — see Architecture
154:   doc §Concurrency).
155: - **Reconnect resilience**: refreshing or losing connection mid-auction must not lose a
156:   Team Owner's identity or their team's purse state — server is the source of truth, not
157:   the browser tab.
158: - **Crash recovery**: timer state is derived from a persisted absolute timestamp, not an
159:   in-memory countdown, so a server restart mid-auction can recover or at minimum fail
160:   gracefully into `PAUSED` rather than corrupting state.
161: 
162: ## 10. Success Criteria / Definition of Done
163: 
164: - A non-technical person can create a room, get 2+ friends to join on their own laptops,
165:   run a full auction of ~10 players, and reach a results page — without you narrating
166:   every click.
167: - Killing and refreshing a Team Owner's tab mid-bid does not break the room for anyone
168:   else.
169: - Demo data/seed flow exists so an evaluator can see a populated room in under a minute.
170: 
171: ## 11. Open Assumptions (restate in your repo README)
172: 
173: 1. Domain = cricket franchise auction (IPL-style), chosen per `iplauction` reference —
174:    swappable by relabeling, not re-architecting.
175: 2. Team Owners don't need persistent accounts; only Commissioners do.
176: 3. Currency is play-money, integer-lakhs internally, ₹ Cr displayed.
177: 4. Default purse ₹100 Cr / team, default squad size 18 — both configurable per room,
178:    not hardcoded business rules.
179: 5. Bid increments follow a simple tiered table we designed for this app (see
180:    Architecture doc) — not a claim about real-world auction rules.
181: 6. Desktop-only, minimum viewport ~1280px, per the assignment's explicit relaxation.

469: ## 5.7. Schema Push Detection Gate
470: 
471: > Detects schema-relevant files in the phase scope and injects a mandatory `[BLOCKING]` schema push task into the plan. Prevents false-positive verification where build/types pass because TypeScript types come from config, not the live database.

481: | ORM | File Patterns |

483: | Payload CMS | `src/collections/**/*.ts`, `src/globals/**/*.ts` |
484: | Prisma | `prisma/schema.prisma`, `prisma/schema/*.prisma` |
485: | Drizzle | `drizzle/schema.ts`, `src/db/schema.ts`, `drizzle/*.ts` |
486: | Supabase | `supabase/migrations/*.sql` |
487: | TypeORM | `src/entities/**/*.ts`, `src/migrations/**/*.ts` |
488: 
489: Also check if any existing PLAN.md files for this phase already reference these file patterns in `files_modified`.
490: 
491: **If schema-relevant files detected:**
492: 
493: Set `SCHEMA_PUSH_REQUIRED=true` and `SCHEMA_ORM={detected_orm}`.
494: 
495: Determine the push command for the detected ORM:
496: 
497: | ORM | Push Command | Non-TTY Workaround |
498: |-----|-------------|-------------------|
499: | Payload CMS | `npx payload migrate` | `CI=true PAYLOAD_MIGRATING=true npx payload migrate` |
500: | Prisma | `npx prisma db push` | `npx prisma db push --accept-data-loss` (if destructive) |
501: | Drizzle | `npx drizzle-kit push` | `npx drizzle-kit push` |
502: | Supabase | `supabase db push` | Set `SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN` env var |
503: | TypeORM | `npx typeorm migration:run` | `npx typeorm migration:run -d src/data-source.ts` |
504: 
505: Inject the following into the planner prompt (step 8) as an additional constraint:
506: 
507: ```markdown
508: <schema_push_requirement>
509: **[BLOCKING] Schema Push Required**
510: 
511: This phase modifies schema-relevant files ({detected_files}). The planner MUST include
512: a `[BLOCKING]` task that runs the database schema push command AFTER all schema file
513: modifications are complete but BEFORE verification.

515: - ORM detected: {SCHEMA_ORM}
516: - Push command: {push_command}
517: - Non-TTY workaround: {env_hint}
518: - If push requires interactive prompts that cannot be suppressed, flag the task for
519:   manual intervention with `autonomous: false`

521: This task is mandatory — the phase CANNOT pass verification without it. Build and
522: type checks will pass without the push (types come from config, not the live database),
523: creating a false-positive verification state.
524: </schema_push_requirement>

624: <downstream_consumer>
625: Output consumed by /gsd-execute-phase. Plans need:
626: - Frontmatter (wave, depends_on, files_modified, autonomous)
627: - Tasks in XML format with read_first and acceptance_criteria fields (MANDATORY on every task)
628: - Verification criteria
629: - must_haves for goal-backward verification
630: </downstream_consumer>
631: 
632: <deep_work_rules>
633: ## Anti-Shallow Execution Rules (MANDATORY)
634: 
635: Every task MUST include these fields — they are NOT optional:
636: 
637: 1. **`<read_first>`** — Files the executor MUST read before touching anything. Always include:
638:    - The file being modified (so executor sees current state, not assumptions)
639:    - Any "source of truth" file referenced in CONTEXT.md (reference implementations, existing patterns, config files, schemas)
640:    - Any file whose patterns, signatures, types, or conventions must be replicated or respected
641: 
642: 2. **`<acceptance_criteria>`** — Verifiable conditions that prove the task was done correctly. Rules:
643:    - Every criterion must be checkable with grep, file read, test command, or CLI output
644:    - NEVER use subjective language ("looks correct", "properly configured", "consistent with")
645:    - ALWAYS include exact strings, patterns, values, or command outputs that must be present
646:    - Examples:
647:      - Code: `auth.py contains def verify_token(` / `test_auth.py exits 0`
648:      - Config: `.env.example contains DATABASE_URL=` / `Dockerfile contains HEALTHCHECK`
649:      - Docs: `README.md contains '## Installation'` / `API.md lists all endpoints`
650:      - Infra: `deploy.yml has rollback step` / `docker-compose.yml has healthcheck for db`
651: 
652: 3. **`<action>`** — Must include CONCRETE values, not references. Rules:
653:    - NEVER say "align X with Y", "match X to Y", "update to be consistent" without specifying the exact target state
654:    - ALWAYS include the actual values: config keys, function signatures, SQL statements, class names, import paths, env vars, etc.
655:    - If CONTEXT.md has a comparison table or expected values, copy them into the action verbatim
656:    - The executor should be able to complete the task from the action text alone, without needing to read CONTEXT.md or reference files (read_first is for verification, not discovery)
657: 
658: **Why this matters:** Executor agents work from the plan text. Vague instructions like "update the config to match production" produce shallow one-line changes. Concrete instructions like "add DATABASE_URL=postgresql://... , set POOL_SIZE=20, add REDIS_URL=redis://..." produce complete work. The cost of verbose plans is far less than the cost of re-doing shallow execution.
659: </deep_work_rules>
660: 
661: <quality_gate>
662: - [ ] PLAN.md files created in phase directory
663: - [ ] Each plan has valid frontmatter
664: - [ ] Tasks are specific and actionable
665: - [ ] Every task has `<read_first>` with at least the file being modified
666: - [ ] Every task has `<acceptance_criteria>` with grep-verifiable conditions
667: - [ ] Every `<action>` contains concrete values (no "align X with Y" without specifying what)
668: - [ ] Dependencies correctly identified
669: - [ ] Waves assigned for parallel execution
670: - [ ] must_haves derived from phase goal
671: </quality_gate>
672: ```

690: ## 9b. Handle Phase Split Recommendation
691: 
692: When the planner returns `## PHASE SPLIT RECOMMENDED`, it means the phase has too many decisions to implement at full fidelity within the plan budget. The planner proposes groupings.
693: 
694: **Extract from planner return:**
695: - Proposed sub-phases (e.g., "17a: processing core (D-01 to D-19)", "17b: billing + config UX (D-20 to D-27)")
696: - Which D-XX decisions go in each sub-phase

699: **Present to user:**

702: 
703: The planner found {N} decisions that cannot all be implemented without
704: simplifying some. Instead of reducing your decisions, we recommend splitting:

706: **Option 1: Split into sub-phases**

708: - Phase {X}b: {name} — {D-XX to D-YY} ({M} decisions)
709: 
710: **Option 2: Proceed anyway** (planner will attempt all, quality may degrade)
711: 
712: **Option 3: Prioritize** — you choose which decisions to implement now,
713: rest become a follow-up phase
714: ```

718: **If "Split":** Use `/gsd-insert-phase` to create the sub-phases, then replan each.
719: **If "Proceed":** Return to planner with instruction to attempt all decisions at full fidelity, accepting more plans/tasks.
720: **If "Prioritize":** Use AskUserQuestion (multiSelect) to let user pick which D-XX are "now" vs "later". Create CONTEXT.md for each sub-phase with the selected decisions.

# Implementation Plan - Phase 2: Live Realtime Auction Room

We will stand up the authoritative realtime auction server and client screens, connecting them via Socket.io. The server is the absolute source of truth: it manages timers, resolves items as SOLD/UNSOLD, enforces purse/size/category caps, and persists all auction events to PostgreSQL.

## User Review Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Timer Management & Server Recovery**
> The countdown timer is managed in-memory on the Socket.io server to allow high-frequency ticks, but it writes `timerEndsAt` (absolute timestamp) to the database on every bid and state transition. If the server crashes or restarts, it can read this timestamp to restore the timer state or gracefully fall back into a `PAUSED` state without data corruption.
>
> **Concurrency Guarantee**
> Bids within the same room are handled sequentially by utilizing a per-room request queue. This guarantees deterministic behavior even if two clients bid on the exact same millisecond.

## Open Questions
- None. Requirements are clear from `docs/PRD.md` and `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`.

---

## Proposed Changes

### Shared Contract Layer

#### [MODIFY] [packages/shared/src/rooms.ts](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/packages/shared/src/rooms.ts)
- Add Zod schemas and TypeScript types for live auction socket payloads:
  - `bidPlaceSchema`: validates `{ amount: number }` (Lakhs).
  - `forceResolveSchema`: validates `{ outcome: 'SOLD' | 'UNSOLD', soldToTeamId?: string, price?: number }`.
  - `roomStateSchema`: extends `lobbySnapshotSchema` to include current item details, timer state (`timerEndsAt`, `secondsRemaining`, `isPaused`), and bids list.

---

### Realtime Auction Server

#### [NEW] [apps/realtime/src/rooms/auction.ts](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/realtime/src/rooms/auction.ts)
- Implement `handleAuctionConnection(socket, user)` to register handlers for live auction events.
- Implement room state handlers:
  - `room:start`: Transitions room status `LOBBY` -> `AUCTION`, sets up the first player (`status = IN_AUCTION`, `auctionOrder = 1`), computes `timerEndsAt`, and starts the server-side countdown.
  - `bid:place`:
    - Validates bid against database rules: current price + minimum increment (derived from `incrementRule`), remaining team purse, maximum squad size (`squadSizeCap`), and category quotas (`roleCaps`).
    - If valid, writes `Bid` record, decrements/updates `Team.purseRemaining`, resets room `timerEndsAt` to `now + timerSeconds`, and broadcasts updated state to all participants.
    - If invalid, emits `bid:rejected` back to the sender.
  - `room:pause` / `room:resume`: Pauses or resumes the active timer. Computes remaining time and saves updated status and timestamps to PostgreSQL.
  - `room:force-resolve`: Allows Commissioner to force-resolve the active player as SOLD or UNSOLD.

#### [NEW] [apps/realtime/src/rooms/timer.ts](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/realtime/src/rooms/timer.ts)
- Implement a server-side active room timer manager:
  - Tracks active timeouts for each room using absolute `timerEndsAt` matching the database.
  - Upon timer expiry:
    - Queries database for the current high bid.
    - If a high bid exists: marks player `status = SOLD`, records `soldToTeamId` and `soldPrice`.
    - If no bid exists: marks player `status = UNSOLD`.
    - Automatically finds the next player (`status = PENDING`, ordered by `auctionOrder`). If one exists, sets them as `IN_AUCTION` and starts a new timer.
    - If no more players are pending: transitions room status to `COMPLETED`.
    - Broadcasts the updated state.

#### [MODIFY] [apps/realtime/src/server.ts](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/realtime/src/server.ts)
- Wire up the new auction and timer management hooks into the connection event.
- Ensure that on startup, any rooms currently marked `AUCTION` in the DB are placed in `PAUSED` or resume their timers depending on whether `timerEndsAt` has already passed.

---

### Web Client

#### [MODIFY] [apps/web/app/room/[code]/lobby/page.tsx](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/web/app/room/[code]/lobby/page.tsx)
- Check `room.status` on connection and `room:state` events.
- If status is `AUCTION` or `PAUSED`, redirect the client to `/room/[code]`.
- Implement `handleStartAuction` by emitting `room:start` to the socket rather than displaying an alert.

#### [NEW] [apps/web/app/room/[code]/page.tsx](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/web/app/room/[code]/page.tsx)
- Create the live auction page matching the Design System specifications:
  - Displays the active player card (name, category, base price, image placeholder).
  - Displays a prominent countdown ring/bar driven by server state.
  - Bid history feed displaying chronological bids.
  - Participant status bar displaying remaining purses for all teams.
  - Interaction controls:
    - **Team Owners**: Display dynamic bid buttons corresponding to the minimum required bid increment.
    - **Commissioner**: Display admin control console with `Pause`, `Resume`, `Force Sold`, `Force Unsold` buttons.
    - **Spectators**: Display view-only state without interactive bidding buttons.
  - Automatically redirects to `/room/[code]/lobby` if the room status reverts to `LOBBY`, and to results if status is `COMPLETED`.

---

## Verification Plan

### Automated Tests
- Write unit tests for bid validation rules (purse check, squad size check, increment check) inside `apps/realtime/src/rooms/` in isolation.
- Run tests:
  ```bash
  pnpm --filter @bidstand/realtime test
  ```

### Manual Verification
- Open 3 browser sessions (e.g. using separate chrome profiles or incognito tabs):
  1. Commissioner (logged in).
  2. Team Owner 1.
  3. Team Owner 2.
- Start the auction from the Commissioner tab.
- Verify active player details and countdown synchronizes instantly across all 3 tabs.
- Place bids from Owner 1 and Owner 2; verify countdown resets on the server and broadcasts the new high bid instantly.
- Test bidding constraints: verify an owner cannot bid above their remaining purse.
- Test timer expiry: let the timer count down to zero and verify the player automatically resolves to `SOLD` (if bids were placed) or `UNSOLD` (if no bids) and advances to the next player.

1: # Bidstand — Product Requirements Document
2: 
3: > Implements **Option 3: Mini Realtime Auction Room** from the take-home assignment.
4: > This document is the seed spec — paste it into `/gsd-new-project` or hand it to whatever
5: > AI coding tool you're driving.
6: 
7: ## 0. One-liner
8: 
9: A realtime auction room where a **Commissioner** runs a live player auction and **Team
10: Owners** bid against each other, under a fixed budget, for a fixed pool of players —
11: until every player is sold or marked unsold.
12: 
13: ## 1. Chosen Domain & Core Assumption
14: 
15: The brief explicitly references `iplauction` as the spiritual model. We are building a
16: **cricket fantasy franchise player auction** (IPL-style):
17: 
18: - Items being auctioned = **Players** (name, role/category, base price, optional photo/stats)
19: - Bidders = **Team Owners**, each representing a **Franchise** with a fixed **Purse**
20:   (budget) and a squad with a max size + role quotas
21: 
22: This is the **stated assumption** the brief asks for. The schema is written generically
23: (`Item`, not `CricketPlayer`) so the same app could be relabeled for sneakers, art, or
24: domain names with no schema change — only seed data and copy would change.
25: 
26: Currency is stored as an **integer in Lakhs** (₹1,00,000 = 1 unit; ₹1 Crore = 100 units)
27: to avoid floating-point bugs. Display formatting (₹X.XX Cr) happens only in the UI layer.
28: 
29: ## 2. Roles
30: 
31: | Role | Description | Identity |
32: |---|---|---|
33: | **Commissioner** (Admin) | Creates rooms, defines the player pool & team purses, starts/pauses/resumes/ends the auction, can force-resolve a stuck item | Real account — email + password |
34: | **Team Owner** (Participant) | Claims one team in a room, bids on the currently active player | Room-scoped session — display name + room code, no account needed |
35: | **Spectator** | Joins to watch only, no bidding rights | Room-scoped session, no team claim |
36: 
37: Why no account for Team Owners: friction-free joining is core to the "feels live" goal —
38: nobody should have to sign up mid-auction to bid. The room-scoped session still
39: satisfies the "auth or session identity" requirement, and it's the realistic UX pattern
40: for this kind of room (Kahoot/Jackbox-style join, not a SaaS login).
41: 
42: ## 3. Goals
43: 
44: - The auction **must feel live**: bids, the current player, the timer, and presence all
45:   update across every open browser tab with no manual refresh.
46: - The auction logic (timer expiry, resolution, validation) is **server-authoritative**.
47:   No client ever decides an outcome; it only ever proposes a bid and renders state the
48:   server pushes back.
49: - The product works end-to-end for a full auction with zero developer hand-holding:
50:   create room → add players & teams → share code → run auction → see results.
51: 
52: ## 4. Non-Goals (v1)
53: 
54: - Mobile responsiveness (assignment explicitly says desktop-only is fine).
55: - Payments / real money — purse is play-money only.
56: - Multi-auction tournaments, trade windows, retained players from "last season."
57: - A public room directory — all rooms are private-by-code by design.
58: 
59: ## 5. Core Features (from the assignment, mapped to this product)
60: 
61: | Brief requirement | Implementation |
62: |---|---|
63: | Create auction room | Commissioner sets name, purse per team, default players, increment rule, timer length |
64: | Join room by code/link | 6-character room code + shareable `/join/[code]` link |
65: | Admin and participant roles | Commissioner / Team Owner / Spectator (see §2) |
66: | Item/player list | Player pool with name, role, base price, photo (optional), status |
67: | Start auction | Commissioner action, moves room `LOBBY → AUCTION` |
68: | Current item/player display | Large "on the block" card, visible to everyone in the room |
69: | Countdown timer | Server-owned absolute `timerEndsAt`; resets on every valid new bid |
70: | Realtime bidding | Socket-based; server validates and broadcasts every accepted bid |
71: | Bid history | Per-player live list: team, amount, timestamp |
72: | Sold/unsold outcome | Timer expiry with no bid → `UNSOLD`; expiry with a leading bid → `SOLD` |
73: | Final results page | Per-team squad, total spend, purse remaining; full unsold list |
74: | Basic room state persistence | Every state transition is written to Postgres, not just kept in memory |
75: 
76: ## 6. Optional Features Included in v1
77: 
78: Chosen because they reinforce the "feels like a real auction" goal and are cheap once
79: sockets + Postgres exist:
80: 
81: - **Team/squad budgets** — purse decreases as a team wins players; can't bid past remaining purse.
82: - **Maximum players per team** — squad size cap (default 18, configurable).
83: - **Role/category caps** — min/max per role (e.g. Batsman/Bowler/All-rounder/Wicketkeeper), configurable per room.
84: - **Auction pause/resume** — Commissioner can pause mid-item; timer freezes and resumes from remaining time.
85: - **Presence indicators** — who's currently connected in the room (small thing, ships almost free once sockets exist).
86: - **Spectator mode** — join without claiming a team.
87: 
88: **Explicitly deferred** to "Future Improvements" (see Known Limitations in README):
89: skip/withdraw voting, chat/reactions, public room directory.
90: 
91: ## 7. Auction State Machine
92: 
93: ```
94: LOBBY ──(commissioner starts)──▶ AUCTION ──(all items resolved)──▶ COMPLETED
95:                                     │  ▲
96:                           (pause)   ▼  │ (resume)
97:                                   PAUSED
98: ```
99: 
100: Within `AUCTION`, each player goes through its own micro state machine:
101: 
102: ```
103: PENDING ──(comes up next)──▶ IN_AUCTION ──(timer expiry, no bid)────▶ UNSOLD
104:                                   │
105:                                   └────(timer expiry, has leading bid)──▶ SOLD
106: ```
107: 
108: Rules:
109: - Exactly one item is `IN_AUCTION` at a time.
110: - A bid is only accepted if: item is `IN_AUCTION`, room is `AUCTION` (not `PAUSED`),
111:   bidder's team has enough remaining purse, bid ≥ current price + required increment,
112:   and the bid arrives before `timerEndsAt`.
113: - Every accepted bid **resets** `timerEndsAt` to `now + timerDuration` (classic
114:   "going once, going twice" pattern). There's a configurable max reset count per item
115:   to guarantee items can't be bid-warred forever (default: unlimited, override-able).
116: - Commissioner can **force-resolve** an item (mark sold to current high bid, or unsold)
117:   if something gets stuck — an escape hatch, not the happy path.
118: 
119: ## 8. Functional Requirements by Screen
120: 
121: **Auth / Landing** — Commissioner sign up / log in. Loading state on submit, inline
122: validation errors, redirect to dashboard on success.
123: 
124: **Commissioner Dashboard** — list of rooms created, "Create Room" CTA, room status
125: badges (Lobby/Live/Completed).
126: 
127: **Create Room Wizard** — room name, default purse per team, number of teams, timer
128: duration, increment rule (flat or tiered — see Architecture doc), squad size cap, role
129: caps, then add players (manual entry or paste/import a list — CSV minimum). Empty state
130: before any players are added; can't start auction with zero players or zero teams.
131: 
132: **Lobby** — room code + shareable link prominently displayed, live list of joined teams
133: (name, owner display name, connected/disconnected dot), Commissioner-only "Start
134: Auction" button (disabled until ≥1 team has joined).
135: 
136: **Auction Room (the core screen)** — current player card (name, role, base price, photo
137: placeholder if none), current highest bid + leading team, countdown ring/bar, bid
138: button(s) per the increment rule, live bid history feed, all teams' purse-remaining +
139: squad-fill bar, Commissioner-only pause/resume/force-resolve controls.
140: 
141: **Results Page** — per-team final squad with prices paid, purse spent vs. remaining,
142: full list of unsold players, shareable/printable summary. Updates live if viewed while
143: the auction is still finishing the last item.
144: 
145: Every screen needs explicit **loading**, **empty**, and **error** states — this is a
146: named evaluation criterion, not a nice-to-have.
147: 
148: ## 9. Non-Functional Requirements
149: 
150: - **Realtime correctness across tabs**: open the same room in 3+ browser tabs/profiles,
151:   bid from each, and all tabs must converge to the same state within ~1 round trip.
152: - **Concurrency**: two teams bidding within the same animation frame must resolve
153:   deterministically (server processes one room's events sequentially — see Architecture
154:   doc §Concurrency).
155: - **Reconnect resilience**: refreshing or losing connection mid-auction must not lose a
156:   Team Owner's identity or their team's purse state — server is the source of truth, not
157:   the browser tab.
158: - **Crash recovery**: timer state is derived from a persisted absolute timestamp, not an
159:   in-memory countdown, so a server restart mid-auction can recover or at minimum fail
160:   gracefully into `PAUSED` rather than corrupting state.
161: 
162: ## 10. Success Criteria / Definition of Done
163: 
164: - A non-technical person can create a room, get 2+ friends to join on their own laptops,
165:   run a full auction of ~10 players, and reach a results page — without you narrating
166:   every click.
167: - Killing and refreshing a Team Owner's tab mid-bid does not break the room for anyone
168:   else.
169: - Demo data/seed flow exists so an evaluator can see a populated room in under a minute.
170: 
171: ## 11. Open Assumptions (restate in your repo README)
172: 
173: 1. Domain = cricket franchise auction (IPL-style), chosen per `iplauction` reference —
174:    swappable by relabeling, not re-architecting.
175: 2. Team Owners don't need persistent accounts; only Commissioners do.
176: 3. Currency is play-money, integer-lakhs internally, ₹ Cr displayed.
177: 4. Default purse ₹100 Cr / team, default squad size 18 — both configurable per room,
178:    not hardcoded business rules.
179: 5. Bid increments follow a simple tiered table we designed for this app (see
180:    Architecture doc) — not a claim about real-world auction rules.
181: 6. Desktop-only, minimum viewport ~1280px, per the assignment's explicit relaxation.

469: ## 5.7. Schema Push Detection Gate
470: 
471: > Detects schema-relevant files in the phase scope and injects a mandatory `[BLOCKING]` schema push task into the plan. Prevents false-positive verification where build/types pass because TypeScript types come from config, not the live database.
472: 
473: Check if any files in the phase scope match schema patterns:
474: 
475: ```bash
476: PHASE_SECTION=$(node "$HOME/.gemini/antigravity/get-shit-done/bin/gsd-tools.cjs" roadmap get-phase "${PHASE}" --pick section 2>/dev/null)
477: ```
478: 
479: Scan `PHASE_SECTION`, `CONTEXT.md` (if loaded), and `RESEARCH.md` (if exists) for file paths matching these ORM patterns:
480: 
481: | ORM | File Patterns |
482: |-----|--------------|
483: | Payload CMS | `src/collections/**/*.ts`, `src/globals/**/*.ts` |
484: | Prisma | `prisma/schema.prisma`, `prisma/schema/*.prisma` |
485: | Drizzle | `drizzle/schema.ts`, `src/db/schema.ts`, `drizzle/*.ts` |
486: | Supabase | `supabase/migrations/*.sql` |
487: | TypeORM | `src/entities/**/*.ts`, `src/migrations/**/*.ts` |
488: 
489: Also check if any existing PLAN.md files for this phase already reference these file patterns in `files_modified`.
490: 
491: **If schema-relevant files detected:**
492: 
493: Set `SCHEMA_PUSH_REQUIRED=true` and `SCHEMA_ORM={detected_orm}`.
494: 
495: Determine the push command for the detected ORM:
496: 
497: | ORM | Push Command | Non-TTY Workaround |
498: |-----|-------------|-------------------|
499: | Payload CMS | `npx payload migrate` | `CI=true PAYLOAD_MIGRATING=true npx payload migrate` |
500: | Prisma | `npx prisma db push` | `npx prisma db push --accept-data-loss` (if destructive) |
501: | Drizzle | `npx drizzle-kit push` | `npx drizzle-kit push` |
502: | Supabase | `supabase db push` | Set `SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN` env var |
503: | TypeORM | `npx typeorm migration:run` | `npx typeorm migration:run -d src/data-source.ts` |
504: 
505: Inject the following into the planner prompt (step 8) as an additional constraint:
506: 
507: ```markdown
508: <schema_push_requirement>
509: **[BLOCKING] Schema Push Required**
510: 
511: This phase modifies schema-relevant files ({detected_files}). The planner MUST include
512: a `[BLOCKING]` task that runs the database schema push command AFTER all schema file
513: modifications are complete but BEFORE verification.
514: 
515: - ORM detected: {SCHEMA_ORM}
516: - Push command: {push_command}
517: - Non-TTY workaround: {env_hint}
518: - If push requires interactive prompts that cannot be suppressed, flag the task for
519:   manual intervention with `autonomous: false`
520: 
521: This task is mandatory — the phase CANNOT pass verification without it. Build and
522: type checks will pass without the push (types come from config, not the live database),
523: creating a false-positive verification state.
524: </schema_push_requirement>
525: ```
526: 
527: Display: `Schema files detected ({SCHEMA_ORM}) — [BLOCKING] push task will be injected into plans`
528: 
529: **If no schema-relevant files detected:** Skip silently to step 6.

632: <deep_work_rules>
633: ## Anti-Shallow Execution Rules (MANDATORY)

635: Every task MUST include these fields — they are NOT optional:

637: 1. **`<read_first>`** — Files the executor MUST read before touching anything. Always include:
638:    - The file being modified (so executor sees current state, not assumptions)
639:    - Any "source of truth" file referenced in CONTEXT.md (reference implementations, existing patterns, config files, schemas)
640:    - Any file whose patterns, signatures, types, or conventions must be replicated or respected
641: 
642: 2. **`<acceptance_criteria>`** — Verifiable conditions that prove the task was done correctly. Rules:
643:    - Every criterion must be checkable with grep, file read, test command, or CLI output
644:    - NEVER use subjective language ("looks correct", "properly configured", "consistent with")
645:    - ALWAYS include exact strings, patterns, values, or command outputs that must be present
646:    - Examples:
647:      - Code: `auth.py contains def verify_token(` / `test_auth.py exits 0`
648:      - Config: `.env.example contains DATABASE_URL=` / `Dockerfile contains HEALTHCHECK`
649:      - Docs: `README.md contains '## Installation'` / `API.md lists all endpoints`
650:      - Infra: `deploy.yml has rollback step` / `docker-compose.yml has healthcheck for db`
651: 
652: 3. **`<action>`** — Must include CONCRETE values, not references. Rules:
653:    - NEVER say "align X with Y", "match X to Y", "update to be consistent" without specifying the exact target state
654:    - ALWAYS include the actual values: config keys, function signatures, SQL statements, class names, import paths, env vars, etc.
655:    - If CONTEXT.md has a comparison table or expected values, copy them into the action verbatim
656:    - The executor should be able to complete the task from the action text alone, without needing to read CONTEXT.md or reference files (read_first is for verification, not discovery)
657: 
658: **Why this matters:** Executor agents work from the plan text. Vague instructions like "update the config to match production" produce shallow one-line changes. Concrete instructions like "add DATABASE_URL=postgresql://... , set POOL_SIZE=20, add REDIS_URL=redis://..." produce complete work. The cost of verbose plans is far less than the cost of re-doing shallow execution.
659: </deep_work_rules>

661: <quality_gate>
662: - [ ] PLAN.md files created in phase directory
663: - [ ] Each plan has valid frontmatter
664: - [ ] Tasks are specific and actionable
665: - [ ] Every task has `<read_first>` with at least the file being modified

690: ## 9b. Handle Phase Split Recommendation
691: 
692: When the planner returns `## PHASE SPLIT RECOMMENDED`, it means the phase has too many decisions to implement at full fidelity within the plan budget. The planner proposes groupings.
693: 
694: **Extract from planner return:**
695: - Proposed sub-phases (e.g., "17a: processing core (D-01 to D-19)", "17b: billing + config UX (D-20 to D-27)")
696: - Which D-XX decisions go in each sub-phase
697: - Why the split is necessary (decision count, complexity estimate)
698: 
699: **Present to user:**
700: ```
701: ## Phase {X} is too complex for full-fidelity implementation
702: 
703: The planner found {N} decisions that cannot all be implemented without
704: simplifying some. Instead of reducing your decisions, we recommend splitting:
705: 
706: **Option 1: Split into sub-phases**
707: - Phase {X}a: {name} — {D-XX to D-YY} ({N} decisions)

# Implementation Plan - Phase 2: Live Realtime Auction Room

We will stand up the authoritative realtime auction server and client screens, connecting them via Socket.io. The server is the absolute source of truth: it manages timers, resolves items as SOLD/UNSOLD, enforces purse/size/category caps, and persists all auction events to PostgreSQL.

## User Review Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Timer Management & Server Recovery**
> The countdown timer is managed in-memory on the Socket.io server to allow high-frequency ticks, but it writes `timerEndsAt` (absolute timestamp) to the database on every bid and state transition. If the server crashes or restarts, it can read this timestamp to restore the timer state or gracefully fall back into a `PAUSED` state without data corruption.
>
> **Concurrency Guarantee**
> Bids within the same room are handled sequentially by utilizing a per-room request queue. This guarantees deterministic behavior even if two clients bid on the exact same millisecond.

## Open Questions
- None. Requirements are clear from `docs/PRD.md` and `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`.

---

## Proposed Changes

### Shared Contract Layer

#### [MODIFY] [packages/shared/src/rooms.ts](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/packages/shared/src/rooms.ts)
- Add Zod schemas and TypeScript types for live auction socket payloads:
  - `bidPlaceSchema`: validates `{ amount: number }` (Lakhs).
  - `forceResolveSchema`: validates `{ outcome: 'SOLD' | 'UNSOLD', soldToTeamId?: string, price?: number }`.
  - `roomStateSchema`: extends `lobbySnapshotSchema` to include current item details, timer state (`timerEndsAt`, `secondsRemaining`, `isPaused`), and bids list.

---

### Realtime Auction Server

#### [NEW] [apps/realtime/src/rooms/auction.ts](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/realtime/src/rooms/auction.ts)
- Implement `handleAuctionConnection(socket, user)` to register handlers for live auction events.
- Implement room state handlers:
  - `room:start`: Transitions room status `LOBBY` -> `AUCTION`, sets up the first player (`status = IN_AUCTION`, `auctionOrder = 1`), computes `timerEndsAt`, and starts the server-side countdown.
  - `bid:place`:
    - Validates bid against database rules: current price + minimum increment (derived from `incrementRule`), remaining team purse, maximum squad size (`squadSizeCap`), and category quotas (`roleCaps`).
    - If valid, writes `Bid` record, decrements/updates `Team.purseRemaining`, resets room `timerEndsAt` to `now + timerSeconds`, and broadcasts updated state to all participants.
    - If invalid, emits `bid:rejected` back to the sender.
  - `room:pause` / `room:resume`: Pauses or resumes the active timer. Computes remaining time and saves updated status and timestamps to PostgreSQL.
  - `room:force-resolve`: Allows Commissioner to force-resolve the active player as SOLD or UNSOLD.

#### [NEW] [apps/realtime/src/rooms/timer.ts](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/realtime/src/rooms/timer.ts)
- Implement a server-side active room timer manager:
  - Tracks active timeouts for each room using absolute `timerEndsAt` matching the database.
  - Upon timer expiry:
    - Queries database for the current high bid.
    - If a high bid exists: marks player `status = SOLD`, records `soldToTeamId` and `soldPrice`.
    - If no bid exists: marks player `status = UNSOLD`.
    - Automatically finds the next player (`status = PENDING`, ordered by `auctionOrder`). If one exists, sets them as `IN_AUCTION` and starts a new timer.
    - If no more players are pending: transitions room status to `COMPLETED`.
    - Broadcasts the updated state.

#### [MODIFY] [apps/realtime/src/server.ts](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/realtime/src/server.ts)
- Wire up the new auction and timer management hooks into the connection event.
- Ensure that on startup, any rooms currently marked `AUCTION` in the DB are placed in `PAUSED` or resume their timers depending on whether `timerEndsAt` has already passed.

---

### Web Client

#### [MODIFY] [apps/web/app/room/[code]/lobby/page.tsx](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/web/app/room/[code]/lobby/page.tsx)
- Check `room.status` on connection and `room:state` events.
- If status is `AUCTION` or `PAUSED`, redirect the client to `/room/[code]`.
- Implement `handleStartAuction` by emitting `room:start` to the socket rather than displaying an alert.

#### [NEW] [apps/web/app/room/[code]/page.tsx](file:///home/varad/Documents/internship-tasks/11-auction-Auction-Task/apps/web/app/room/[code]/page.tsx)
- Create the live auction page matching the Design System specifications:
  - Displays the active player card (name, category, base price, image placeholder).
  - Displays a prominent countdown ring/bar driven by server state.
  - Bid history feed displaying chronological bids.
  - Participant status bar displaying remaining purses for all teams.
  - Interaction controls:
    - **Team Owners**: Display dynamic bid buttons corresponding to the minimum required bid increment.
    - **Commissioner**: Display admin control console with `Pause`, `Resume`, `Force Sold`, `Force Unsold` buttons.
    - **Spectators**: Display view-only state without interactive bidding buttons.
  - Automatically redirects to `/room/[code]/lobby` if the room status reverts to `LOBBY`, and to results if status is `COMPLETED`.

---

## Verification Plan

### Automated Tests
- Write unit tests for bid validation rules (purse check, squad size check, increment check) inside `apps/realtime/src/rooms/` in isolation.
- Run tests:
  ```bash
  pnpm --filter @bidstand/realtime test
  ```

### Manual Verification
- Open 3 browser sessions (e.g. using separate chrome profiles or incognito tabs):
  1. Commissioner (logged in).
  2. Team Owner 1.
  3. Team Owner 2.
- Start the auction from the Commissioner tab.
- Verify active player details and countdown synchronizes instantly across all 3 tabs.
- Place bids from Owner 1 and Owner 2; verify countdown resets on the server and broadcasts the new high bid instantly.
- Test bidding constraints: verify an owner cannot bid above their remaining purse.
- Test timer expiry: let the timer count down to zero and verify the player automatically resolves to `SOLD` (if bids were placed) or `UNSOLD` (if no bids) and advances to the next player.

1: # Bidstand — Architecture & Tech Stack
2: 
3: ## 1. Stack Summary
4: 
5: | Layer | Choice | Why |
6: |---|---|---|
7: | Frontend | Next.js 14 (App Router) + TypeScript | One framework for landing/dashboard/auction UI, good Vercel deploy story |
8: | Styling/UI | Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui | Fast to get clean, consistent UI without bespoke CSS |
9: | Auth | Auth.js (NextAuth) — Credentials provider | Real accounts for Commissioners only; bcrypt-hashed passwords |
10: | Realtime | Socket.io on a standalone Node + Express server | Full control over timer authority, room semantics, presence — see §3 |
11: | Database | PostgreSQL (Neon, serverless-friendly) | Free tier, branching for dev/preview, plays well with Prisma |
12: | ORM | Prisma | Type-safe schema shared by both the web app and the realtime server |
13: | Frontend deploy | Vercel | Native Next.js hosting |
14: | Realtime server deploy | Railway or Render | Persistent Node process — required for Socket.io + long-lived timers; Vercel serverless functions cannot hold a websocket connection open |
15: | Monorepo tool | pnpm workspaces (or Turborepo if you want build caching) | Keeps frontend/backend/shared types in one repo without a build framework fight |
16: 
17: This satisfies the assignment's "clear separation between frontend, backend, database,
18: and realtime logic" requirement structurally, not just by convention.
19: 
20: ## 2. Repository Layout
21: 
22: ```
23: bidstand/
24: ├── apps/
25: │   ├── web/                 # Next.js app (frontend + REST-ish API routes)
26: │   │   ├── app/
27: │   │   ├── lib/
28: │   │   └── ...
29: │   └── realtime/             # Express + Socket.io server
30: │       ├── src/
31: │       │   ├── server.ts
32: │       │   ├── rooms/        # per-room state machine, in-memory + DB-backed
33: │       │   └── events/       # socket event handlers
34: │       └── ...
35: ├── packages/
36: │   ├── db/                   # Prisma schema + generated client, shared by both apps
37: │   └── shared/                # Zod schemas, shared TS types, socket event contracts
38: ├── docs/                      # this doc set
39: ├── ai-transcripts/
40: ├── CLAUDE.md
41: ├── AGENTS.md
42: └── README.md
43: ```
44: 
45: Why a separate `realtime` app instead of Next.js API routes / route handlers: Next on
46: Vercel runs API routes as short-lived serverless functions, which cannot hold a
47: persistent websocket connection or an in-process countdown timer reliably. A small
48: always-on Node process solves this with one job — own the live auction state.
49: 
50: ## 3. Realtime Design
51: 
52: ### 3.1 Socket rooms
53: One Socket.io room per `roomCode`. Every connected client (Commissioner, Team Owner,
54: Spectator) joins that room on connect; nothing is broadcast outside it.
55: 
56: ### 3.2 Events (contract lives in `packages/shared`)
57: 
58: | Event | Direction | Payload | Notes |
59: |---|---|---|---|
60: | `room:join` | client → server | `{ roomCode, sessionToken }` | Validates session, joins socket room |
61: | `room:state` | server → client | full room snapshot | Sent on join and on any major transition |
62: | `auction:start` / `pause` / `resume` | client(admin) → server | `{ roomCode }` | Commissioner-only, server checks role |
63: | `item:current` | server → client | current item + `timerEndsAt` (ISO) | Sent when a new item goes live |
64: | `bid:place` | client → server | `{ roomCode, itemId, teamId, amount }` | Server validates, never trusts client math |
65: | `bid:accepted` | server → client | `{ itemId, teamId, amount, timerEndsAt }` | Broadcast to whole room; resets timer |
66: | `bid:rejected` | server → client | `{ reason }` | Sent only to the bidder, e.g. "purse exceeded" |
67: | `item:resolved` | server → client | `{ itemId, outcome: 'SOLD'|'UNSOLD', teamId?, amount? }` | |
68: | `auction:completed` | server → client | final results payload | |
69: | `presence:update` | server → client | connected participant list | |
70: 
71: ### 3.3 Timer authority
72: 
73: The single most important correctness rule in this app:
74: 
75: - The realtime server is the **only** thing that decides when an item resolves.
76: - Timer state is an absolute timestamp, `timerEndsAt`, not a relative "15 seconds left."
77:   Clients compute `remaining = timerEndsAt - Date.now()` locally and just re-render —
78:   this avoids drift from network latency and survives tab refreshes.
79: - The server runs one `setTimeout`/interval per active item, keyed off the same
80:   `timerEndsAt` it broadcast. On expiry it re-checks the canonical in-memory state
81:   (current highest bid) before resolving — so even if a bid landed in the same tick as
82:   expiry, resolution is based on actual accepted state, not a race.
83: - `timerEndsAt` is persisted to the `Room` row on every change, so a server restart can
84:   at minimum recover into a sane `PAUSED` state instead of silently corrupting an
85:   in-progress item.
86: 
87: ### 3.4 Concurrency: simultaneous bids
88: 
89: Two Team Owners bidding "at the same time" are never actually simultaneous once they
90: reach the server — Node's event loop processes socket events one at a time, and each
91: room's state lives in one process. The handler for `bid:place`:
92: 
93: 1. Validate against the in-memory canonical state (current price, item status, team
94:    purse, increment rule) — reject fast if it's stale.
95: 2. If valid, update in-memory state **synchronously**, write the bid row to Postgres,
96:    then broadcast `bid:accepted`.
97: 3. The second bidder's event is processed *after* step 2 finishes, sees the new current
98:    price, and is rejected or accepted against the updated number.
99: 
100: This means a simple in-process check is sufficient — no database-level locking is
101: required because there is exactly one authoritative process per room. (If this were
102: ever scaled to multiple realtime server instances, you'd need a per-room lock, e.g. via
103: Redis — explicitly out of scope for v1, noted as a Known Limitation.)
104: 
105: ## 4. Auth Design
106: 
107: - **Commissioners**: real accounts, Auth.js Credentials provider, bcrypt-hashed
108:   passwords, session via JWT cookie.
109: - **Team Owners / Spectators**: on joining a room, the web app issues a short-lived,
110:   room-scoped JWT (`{ roomId, participantId, role, teamId? }`) stored in an httpOnly
111:   cookie *and* handed to the socket connection during the handshake (`auth: { token }`).
112:   The realtime server verifies this token on every socket connection and on every
113:   privileged event (e.g. only the Commissioner's token can call `auction:start`).
114: - A `localStorage` anonId persists alongside the cookie so a refreshed tab can re-claim
115:   the same participant row instead of creating a duplicate "ghost" participant.
116: 
117: ## 5. Deployment Topology
118: 
119: ```
120: Browser ──HTTPS──▶ Vercel (Next.js: pages, API routes for CRUD)
121:    │                         │
122:    │                         ▼
123:    │                   Postgres (Neon)
124:    │                         ▲
125:    └──WebSocket───▶ Railway/Render (Socket.io server) ──┘
126: ```
127: 
128: REST-ish operations (create room, add players, signup/login, fetch results) go through
129: Next.js API routes / route handlers, talking to Postgres via Prisma. Anything that needs
130: to be "live" goes through the Socket.io server, which talks to the *same* Postgres
131: database via the same Prisma schema (shared package).
132: 
133: ## 6. Environment Variables
134: 
135: ```
136: # apps/web/.env
137: DATABASE_URL=postgresql://...
138: NEXTAUTH_SECRET=...
139: NEXTAUTH_URL=https://your-app.vercel.app
140: NEXT_PUBLIC_REALTIME_URL=https://your-realtime.up.railway.app
141: ROOM_JWT_SECRET=...
142: 
143: # apps/realtime/.env
144: DATABASE_URL=postgresql://...
145: ROOM_JWT_SECRET=...           # must match web's value
146: ALLOWED_ORIGIN=https://your-app.vercel.app
147: PORT=4000
148: ```
149: 
150: `ROOM_JWT_SECRET` must be shared between both apps since both mint/verify the
151: room-scoped tokens. Document this clearly in `.env.example` so it's not missed.
152: 
153: ## 7. Error Handling Baseline
154: 
155: - Every Prisma call in an API route / socket handler is wrapped; failures return a
156:   typed error shape (`{ ok: false, code, message }`), never a raw stack trace to the
157:   client.
158: - Socket disconnects are handled explicitly (`presence:update` on disconnect, participant
159:   marked "disconnected" not deleted, so purse/team state survives).
160: - Client-side: a single `SocketProvider` exposes connection status (`connecting | open |
161:   reconnecting | closed`) so the UI can show a reconnecting banner instead of silently
162:   freezing.
163:

34: ### ROOM-01: Commissioner Room Creation
35: 
36: Room creation needs a real Commissioner account, a persisted `Room`, configured `Team` rows, and persisted `Item` rows. The minimum create-room surface should support:
37: 
38: - room name
39: - default purse per team, stored as integer lakhs
40: - timer duration in seconds
41: - increment rule as JSON
42: - squad size cap
43: - role caps as JSON
44: - team names
45: - player pool with name, category, base price, optional image URL, and auction order

47: The plan should include server-side validation for all create-room inputs. Client forms may improve usability, but the API route must remain authoritative for accepted shapes and constraints.

49: ### ROOM-02: Join by Room Code or Link
50: 
51: The system needs a six-character room code and a join flow at `/join/[code]`. Joining should not rely on public room directories or search. Invalid codes need a friendly recoverable state, not an unhandled error.
52: 
53: The join API should create or reattach a `Participant` based on a browser-scoped `anonId` and the target room. The web app should issue a room-scoped JWT after successful join so Phase 2 can reuse the same session model for sockets.
54: 
55: ### ROOM-03: Role Claiming
56: 
57: Phase 1 must distinguish:
58: 
59: - Commissioner: authenticated real account that owns the room.
60: - Team Owner: room-scoped participant claiming exactly one available `Team`.
61: - Spectator: room-scoped participant with no team and no bidding rights.
62: 
63: Team claims must be checked server-side. A hidden UI control is not access control. Team Owner joins must reject already claimed teams unless the same `anonId` is reattaching to its existing participant/team relationship.
64: 
65: ### TECH-01: PostgreSQL Persistence
66: 
67: The Prisma schema from `docs/DATABASE_SCHEMA.md` should be the source of truth, with currency stored as integer lakhs. Phase 1 should include enough database lifecycle setup to run locally:
68: 
69: - workspace package for Prisma
70: - schema enums/models
71: - generated client export
72: - `.env.example` placeholders
73: - migration/dev command wired through `pnpm`
74: 
75: If local PostgreSQL/Neon is not available during execution, the executor should still create schema/config and document the exact migration command to run.

81: | Capability | Primary Tier | Secondary Tier | Rationale |
82: |------------|--------------|----------------|-----------|
83: | Commissioner authentication | `apps/web` API routes | `packages/db` | Credentials and password hashes must be handled server-side against persisted `User` rows. |
84: | Room creation | `apps/web` API routes | `packages/shared`, `packages/db` | API validates create-room payloads with shared Zod schemas and persists room/team/item rows in one transaction. |
85: | Room code joining | `apps/web` API routes | `packages/shared`, `packages/db` | Join requests are untrusted client input; the API must validate role/team claims and persist/reattach participants. |
86: | Room-scoped JWT minting | `apps/web` API routes | `packages/shared` | The web server issues signed room-scoped tokens after database-backed authorization checks. |
87: | Lobby presence broadcasting | `apps/realtime` | `packages/db`, `packages/shared` | Socket server verifies room-scoped JWTs, joins Socket.io rooms, updates participant presence, and broadcasts canonical lobby state. |
88: | Lobby rendering | `apps/web` UI | `apps/realtime` | Browser renders room code, team claims, participant presence, and connection state; it never asserts authority. |
89: | Persistent auction entities | `packages/db` | `apps/web`, `apps/realtime` | Prisma schema is shared by both server apps and remains the source of truth for rooms, teams, items, bids, and participants. |

# Phase 2: Live Realtime Auction Room - Research

**Researched:** 2026-06-26
**Phase:** 2 - Live Realtime Auction Room
**Goal:** Authoritative bidding, timers, socket server, and state transitions
**Requirements:** ROOM-04, ROOM-05, PLAY-01, PLAY-02, BID-01, BID-02, BID-03, TECH-02

## Research Complete

Phase 2 moves the Bidstand project from static CRUD/lobby views into the live, interactive auction experience. The realtime Socket.io server must own the timer, validate bid parameters synchronously against the database and state rules, and broadcast status transitions. The client renders this server-driven state instantly.

## Canonical References

The planner and executor must read these before implementing Phase 2:
- `AGENTS.md` - coding standards, typescript, Zod contracts, and security rules.
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` - socket rooms, timer authority, concurrency control, and jwt token validation.
- `docs/DATABASE_SCHEMA.md` - models for `Room`, `Item`, `Bid`, `Team`, `Participant`.
- `docs/USER_FLOWS.md` - screens, state machine transition triggers, and edge cases.
- `docs/DESIGN_SYSTEM.md` - Design instructions for the Auction Room UI.

## Scope Interpretation

### ROOM-04: Room State Transitions
- Room transitions from `LOBBY` -> `AUCTION` -> `COMPLETED`.
- Within the `AUCTION` status:
  - If no current item exists: the server selects the first pending item (`status = PENDING` by `auctionOrder` asc), updates its status to `IN_AUCTION`, sets it as `currentItemId` in the `Room` row, sets `timerEndsAt = now + timerSeconds`, and broadcasts.
  - If all items are resolved: Room status transitions to `COMPLETED`.

### ROOM-05: Pause and Resume
- The Commissioner can pause the auction.
- When paused:
  - Room status changes to `PAUSED` (or room status remains `AUCTION` but `timerEndsAt` is set to null, and `secondsRemaining` is computed and stored). Let's use the DB `status = PAUSED` enum value!
  - The server timer is cleared.
  - When resumed, status changes back to `AUCTION`, `timerEndsAt` is recalculated as `now + secondsRemaining`, and the server timer is restarted.

### PLAY-01 & PLAY-02: Player Display & Bid History
- Active player card renders: Name, role (category), base price, current highest bid (with leading team), and timer.
- Live bid history feed lists bids chronologically (newest first).

### BID-01: Realtime Socket Bidding & Validation
Bids are sent via `bid:place`. A bid is rejected unless:
1. Room status is `AUCTION` (not `PAUSED` or `LOBBY`).
2. Item status is `IN_AUCTION`.
3. Bidding team has sufficient remaining purse (`purseRemaining >= amount`).
4. Bid amount is greater than or equal to `currentPrice + increment` (current price is current highest bid, or base price if no bids).
5. The bid is submitted by a verified member of that team (authenticated via the room-scoped JWT).
6. The bid arrives before `timerEndsAt` has passed on the server.
7. Squad cap check: the team has not reached `squadSizeCap`.
8. Role quota check: the team has not exceeded the max quota for that player's role (category).

### BID-02 & BID-03: Timer and Resolution
- Every valid bid resets `timerEndsAt` to `now + timerSeconds`.
- Upon timer expiry, the server resolves the item:
  - If high bid exists: status = `SOLD`, records `soldToTeamId` and `soldPrice`, updates `Team.purseRemaining = Team.purseRemaining - soldPrice`.
  - If no bid exists: status = `UNSOLD`.
  - Automatically loads the next player or marks the room `COMPLETED` if none remain.

## Implementation Architecture

### Architectural Responsibility Map

| Capability | Primary Tier | Secondary Tier | Rationale |
|------------|--------------|----------------|-----------|
| Socket Auth | `apps/realtime` | `packages/shared` | Decodes room-scoped JWT token during handshake. |
| State Machine | `apps/realtime` | `packages/db` | Express-Socket.io server manages countdown loops and transitions room/item states in PostgreSQL. |
| Bidding Rules | `apps/realtime` | `packages/shared`, `packages/db` | Server processes bids sequentially, validates math/purses, and commits bids to database. |
| Client UI | `apps/web` | `apps/realtime` | Next.js client renders countdowns, active card, history feed, and sends action proposals to the socket server. |

1: # Bidstand — Design System
2: 
3: Desktop-only per the assignment's explicit relaxation. Design for a **minimum 1280px**
4: viewport; don't burn time on responsive breakpoints.
5: 
6: ## 1. Direction
7: 
8: A live auction needs to *feel* live — energy, tension, a clear "moment" when something
9: sells. Lean into a **stadium scoreboard / broadcast graphics** aesthetic rather than a
10: generic SaaS dashboard:
11: 
12: - Dark base (near-black navy/charcoal), not pure black — gives accent colors room to pop.
13: - One confident accent color for "active/live" state (amber/gold reads as "auction" —
14:   think hammer-falling energy), green for SOLD, a muted red/slate for UNSOLD (not alarm
15:   red — unsold isn't an error, it's a normal outcome).
16: - Tabular/monospace numerals for currency and the timer specifically — anything that
17:   changes rapidly (price ticking up, countdown) should not visually reflow as digit
18:   widths change.
19: - A bold, condensed display weight for the "on the block" player name — this is the one
20:   moment the design should shout.
21: 
22: ## 2. Palette (CSS variables)
23: 
24: ```css
25: --bg-base: #0B0F17;        /* page background */
26: --bg-surface: #131826;     /* cards, panels */
27: --bg-surface-raised: #1B2233;
28: --border-subtle: #262E40;
29: 
30: --text-primary: #F4F6FA;
31: --text-secondary: #9AA4B8;
32: --text-muted: #5C667A;
33: 
34: --accent-live: #F5B83D;    /* active auction / "on the block" */
35: --accent-sold: #3DDC84;
36: --accent-unsold: #8893A6;  /* deliberately muted, not red */
37: --accent-danger: #E3564B;  /* real errors only */
38: 
39: --font-display: 'Archivo', sans-serif;     /* condensed, bold — player name */
40: --font-body: 'Inter', sans-serif;
41: --font-mono: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace;   /* currency, timer */
42: ```
43: 
44: (Swap exact font names for whatever's available — the point is: one condensed display
45: face, one clean body face, one tabular mono face for numbers.)
46: 
47: ## 3. Pages & Layout
48: 
49: ### Landing / Auth
50: Centered card, minimal — this page's only job is to get a Commissioner to a dashboard
51: fast. No need for marketing flourish.
52: 
53: ### Commissioner Dashboard
54: Left-aligned list of room cards (name, status badge, created date) + a prominent
55: "New Room" button top-right. Empty state is a single centered illustration-less prompt,
56: not a sparse table.
57: 
58: ### Create Room Wizard
59: Three-step horizontal stepper at the top (Basics → Teams → Players). Keep all fields on
60: one scroll per step rather than nested modals — Commissioners will be doing this once
61: per auction, optimize for "fill it in fast," not for visual delight.
62: 
63: ### Lobby
64: Two-column: left = room code/link in a large card with a copy button; right = live
65: roster (teams claimed/unclaimed, participant presence list below). Commissioner's
66: "Start Auction" CTA is fixed bottom-right, large, can't be missed.
67: 
68: ### Auction Room (the centerpiece screen)
69: This is the screen that needs to feel good. Suggested layout, three columns:
70: 
71: ```
72: ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
73: │  [Team purse strip — all teams, remaining purse, fill bar] │
74: ├───────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┤
75: │                       │  Bid history (live feed,           │
76: │   CURRENT PLAYER      │  newest on top, team + amount +    │
77: │   CARD                │  relative time)                    │
78: │   - name (display     │                                     │
79: │     font, large)      │                                     │
80: │   - role tag           │                                     │
81: │   - base price         │                                     │
82: │   - current price      │                                     │
83: │     (mono, large)      │                                     │
84: │   - leading team        │                                     │
85: │   - countdown ring      │                                     │
86: │   - bid button(s)       │                                     │
87: ├───────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┤
88: │  [Commissioner controls — pause/resume/force-resolve]      │
89: │  (hidden entirely for non-admin roles, not just disabled)   │
90: └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
91: ```
92: 
93: - **Countdown**: an actual ring or bar, not just a number — peripheral-vision-readable
94:   urgency matters more here than precision.
95: - **SOLD/UNSOLD moment**: a short full-card overlay transition (color wash + the
96:   resolution amount in large mono type) before sliding to the next player — this is the
97:   single highest-leverage animation in the whole app for "feeling live."
98: - **Bid buttons**: show the actual next valid amount, not a generic "+" — e.g. one big
99:   primary button "Bid ₹1.10 Cr," not a stepper a user has to interpret.
100: 
101: ### Results Page
102: Grid of team cards (squad list + spend bar), an "Unsold" section below, a subtle
103: celebratory treatment for the page itself (not per-team — keep it neutral across teams,
104: this isn't declaring a "winner").
105: 
106: ## 4. Component Inventory
107: 
108: - `RoomCodeBadge` — large monospace code + copy button
109: - `CountdownRing` — SVG ring, props: `endsAt`, `durationSeconds`
110: - `PlayerCard` (current item) — has explicit loading skeleton variant for the moment
111:   between items
112: - `BidHistoryFeed` — virtualized if it gets long, newest-first
113: - `TeamPurseStrip` — horizontal scroller of compact team chips, fill bar per team
114: - `ResolutionOverlay` — SOLD/UNSOLD transition
115: - `PresenceDot` — connected/disconnected/idle states
116: - `ToastStack` — for bid-rejected reasons, connection issues
117: - `EmptyState` — reusable, icon + message + optional action, used across dashboard/
118:   lobby/results
119: 
120: ## 5. States Checklist (apply to every data-driven view)
121: 
122: - **Loading**: skeletons that match real layout shape, never a bare spinner on a screen
123:   that has structure (auction room, results).
124: - **Empty**: a sentence + an action, never a blank white/dark void.
125: - **Error**: specific enough to act on ("Couldn't reach the auction server — retrying…"
126:   not "Something went wrong"), with a retry action where one makes sense.
127: - **Disconnected** (auction-room specific): a non-blocking top banner, bidding controls
128:   disabled while it's shown, auto-clears on reconnect.

# Phase 2: Live Realtime Auction Room - UI Design Contract

**Phase:** 2 - Live Realtime Auction Room
**Theme:** Stadium Scoreboard / Broadcast Graphics

## Layout Structure

The Live Auction screen (`/room/[code]`) is desktop-first (min-width: 1280px) and structured into three primary layout zones:

1. **Top Zone: Team Purse Strip**
   - Horizontal row showing all teams in the room.
   - For each team: displays Team Name, owner name, remaining purse (e.g. `₹85.50 Cr` / `₹100.00 Cr`), and a progress bar representing the percentage of budget remaining.
   
2. **Main Content Zone (Two Columns)**
   - **Left Column: Current Player Card & Bidding Controls**
     - Renders details of the player currently up for auction (`IN_AUCTION`).
     - Display name in large, bold font (`--font-display`).
     - Category/role tag (e.g. `Batsman`, `Bowler`, `All-rounder`, `Wicketkeeper`).
     - Base price and current highest bid (with leading team name) in large monospace font (`--font-mono`).
     - Countdown ring or bar showing seconds remaining (derived from `timerEndsAt`).
     - Bidding buttons:
       - **Primary Bid Button**: Shows the exact amount of the next valid bid (e.g. `Bid ₹1.10 Cr` based on the active increment rule).
       - Disabled if the team owner doesn't have enough purse, has filled their squad size cap, or has filled their category cap.
   - **Right Column: Live Bid History Feed**
     - Chronological list of bids placed on the current player (newest first).
     - Each entry shows: team owner name, bid amount, and a relative timestamp.

3. **Bottom Zone: Commissioner Control Console (Commissioner Only)**
   - Actions: `Pause Auction`, `Resume Auction`, `Force Sold`, `Force Unsold`.
   - Hidden completely from Team Owners and Spectators (not just disabled).

## Components Checklist

- [ ] **PurseProgressStrip**: Displays all teams with remaining budget indicators.
- [ ] **ActivePlayerCard**: Displays player image placeholder, name, category, and base price.
- [ ] **LiveBidFeed**: A scrolling list of real-time bids.
- [ ] **CountdownRing**: SVG-based ring showing timer status.
- [ ] **BidButton**: Action button showing the next increment bid amount.
- [ ] **ResolutionOverlay**: Screen-wide green (`SOLD`) or gray (`UNSOLD`) overlay shown briefly when an item resolves.
- [ ] **DisconnectedBanner**: Displayed at the top of the viewport if the websocket disconnects.
